title: RA.1027 JADALAREIGN
tracklist:

Recloose - Soul Clap 2000

translation.content: New York native JADALAREIGN has always represented Black excellence, but in recent years her vision crystallised. The in-demand act has fine-tuned her creative practice, experimenting with tempo and selection in ways that have led to a deep, nuanced relationship with Black artistry, one that centres musical education through storytelling. "It's on the venues, festivals and promoters to ask themselves: 'do we want to make nightlife more equitable for people of colour or are we just doing this for optics because being inclusive is trendy right now?'" In this 2021 RA Exchange, the former Discwoman intern laid out a simple mandate: make real space for marginalised creatives. She put that into practice over five years as one of the bookers at Nowadays, New York's preeminent underground club. Under JADALAREIGN's watch, more under-the-radar artists of colour got Friday night slots and infiltrated Nowadays' prestigious 24-hour parties. Though she left the venue last year, she's still building the kind of dance floor she's always argued for: Her Love From The Sun parties are centred on all-Black lineups, with real heads moving to the likes of Traxx, Analog Soul, SHYBOI and Karizma. As New York's underground clubs draw more mainstream attention, her blueprint feels vital: music and community first. Behind the decks, JADALAREIGN is just as principled. RA.1027 opens with a vocal sample whose message mirrors her wider creative practice: "I'm an African woman who believes in justice for all people. The priorities of this planet have to completely change." From there, the mix ricochets through rumbly drums and sci-fi whirr, peppering house melodies with slo-mo bleeps and techy steppers. She moves across club genres with fluid ease, keeping the cadence loose-limbed yet dynamic throughout. It's strange and tactile—and it sounds like freedom. JADALAREIGN seems surer than ever about all aspects of her career, and it shows in her RA Mix. They say wisdom brings sorrow but if you see her at the function, her joy for her work is ever bountiful. For US Black History Month, it's a timely reminder that history isn't only something we look back on; JADALAREIGN is making it, live. - Nyshka Chandran What have you been up to recently? I've been doing a lot more resting, reading, research, working on my party, Love From The Sun. I've also been doing some ancestral work, building out my family tree, which has been a very powerful experience, and just generally, spending a lot more time outside of the club wherever possible. It's been a necessary and invigorating practice. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? So the mix was recorded here at my home studio in Brooklyn, New York. I used a TRM, 402 rotary mixer, two Technics turntables and two CDJs. It's a snapshot of my eclectic tastes and range as a DJ. There's some house, techno, disco, acid, soul, jack. And I also wanted to represent as many Black American producers as possible. I think that the Black American underground serves as an inspiration for a lot of the music and nightlife culture that we see today, but isn't always platformed in a way that is deserved. So that was very important to me. I also wanted to represent the cities that have inspired me the most, Chicago, Detroit and New York, as well as touching on Black American history, social justice issues, things of that nature. What are you looking forward to in the near future? The fall of empire, indigenous people getting their land back, Black people getting reparations, and the creation of a new reality that is based on love and respect and unity and equality. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? We're living in a really unique moment in history, and there are lots of causes that need and deserve our attention. It's important to start with ourselves and understand that we have power and control over what happens next. As long as we're unified, we have the ability to produce new realities and different outcomes than the ones that we've seen thus far. I think that starts with our local communities, our peers, our neighbours, our friends, supporting each other, supporting local initiatives and businesses, and investing in mutual aid—we are capable of producing a new future together
translation.blurb: Two hours of groove, texture and Black excellence from new-school New York royalty.
